dominant_error_pattern: Learners privilege the sign (positive or negative) of a correlation over its absolute magnitude, believing r=0.97 is stronger than r=-0.98 because of its sign—missing the critical insight that correlation strength is determined by absolute value, not direction.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A selects 0.97, treating positive sign as the strength indicator while ignoring that |-0.98|=0.98 > 0.97. Distractor C (0.1) suggests confusion about magnitude scales entirely. Distractor D (-0.250) compounds the sign bias with even smaller magnitude. Show two scatter plots side by side: one showing r=0.97 (scattered upward-right line) and one showing r=-0.98 (tightly clustered downward-right line). Ask: 'Which relationship is tighter? Which correlation is stronger?' Hammer home: 'Negative and positive tell us direction. Magnitude tells us strength. Always use absolute value.'

dominant_error_pattern: Experimental groups are conflated with methodological terminology—students name the group exposed to treatment as 'independent' (a variable property, not a group label), 'dependent' (a measurement outcome, not a participant group), or 'control' (the comparison group, not the one receiving treatment).
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B uses 'independent group,' a non-standard term that mirrors 'independent variable' terminology, confusing variable types with group roles. Distractor C names the treatment-exposed group as 'control,' inverting the definition entirely. Distractor D applies 'dependent variable' (the outcome measure) to the group itself, conflating measurement with participants. Create a three-box diagram: Group A box, Group B box, Treatment arrow. Label: 'Group A = Experimental (gets treatment). Group B = Control (no treatment). What they measure = Dependent variable. What we manipulate = Independent variable.' Have students label boxes until automatic.

dominant_error_pattern: Independent variables are conflated with non-standard terminology ('experimental variable,' 'manipulation variable') or with the dependent variable itself—missing that independent variable is the precise term for the manipulated factor whose effect is being tested, while dependent variable is what is measured.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A invents 'experimental variable,' which blurs the established independent-variable terminology and suggests the variable is somehow tied to the experimental group rather than to what is being manipulated across groups. Distractor B uses 'manipulation variable,' a descriptive phrase that is not standard nomenclature and delays students' mastery of 'independent variable' as the canonical term. Distractor D (dependent variable) directly inverts the relationship, naming what is measured rather than what is controlled. Teach by building a cause-effect sentence: 'Music (independent—we control it) causes better performance (dependent—we measure it). Say it three times. Then map it to your experiment structure.'
